title: India’s rice fields now rank among world’s top methane hotspots
content:
Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from paddy fields worldwide have doubled since the 1960s, a new study in NatureFood has found. In the most recent decade, 2011-2020, paddy fields around the world produced around 1.1 billion tonnes of carbon dioxide-equivalent (CO2-e) emissions every year — a 90.4% increase from the annual emissions between 1961 and 1980, the study reported.
Upon examining the sources contributing to the rise in emissions, the study found that about half the increase was driven by the loss of soil organic carbon. Between 1961 and 1980, paddy fields were soil organic carbon sinks, removing some 289 million tonnes of CO2-e emissions annually. However, the sink weakened over time. In the 2010s, over a third of paddy fields worldwide in fact began losing carbon from the soil.
Apart from soil organic carbon, methane from paddy fields was also a significant driver of emissions. Flooded fields develop low-oxygen conditions where methane-producing microbes thrive. And paddy fields in eastern India were among the global methane hotspots.
“Most previous studies have focused on a single greenhouse gas, a specific management practice or a particular region,” Hanqin Tian, co-author of the study and director and institute professor, Department of Earth and Environmental Sciences, Boston College, the U.S., said.
“Our study provides a much more comprehensive assessment by integrating a global meta-analysis of more than 1,200 field experiments, process-based ecosystem modelling, and artificial intelligence approaches to quantify GHG emissions from rice cultivation worldwide.”
India tops the list of countries on rice production, delivering 154 million tonnes in 2025-26. The area under paddy cultivation has also increased by around 25% since the 1970s, and currently covers 55% of India’s rainfed harvested area. This expansion, together with continuous irrigation of fields, incorporating residues such as leftover stalks, leaves, and stubble, and increasing use of nitrogen fertilisers, also increased emissions.
Considering rice’s crucial role in India’s food security, the study underscored the need for urgent measures to reduce emissions while ensuring sustainable production.
“Our analysis indicates that a combination of improved irrigation, optimised fertiliser use, residue management, and reduced tillage could reduce global rice-related greenhouse gas emissions by about 10% by mid-century, even under a warming climate,” Dr. Tian said.
The study also projected that between 2031 and 2050, annual emissions will increase by around 26%, fuelled by global warming, erratic rainfall, and the continued intense use of agricultural inputs.
Irrigated paddy fields in India release around 3.9 million tonnes of methane every year. This is a little more than the entire annual methane emissions of Germany.
While India has not set up goals to reduce emissions from agriculture, several techniques and incentives are currently available to farmers.
The National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA) project, for example, suggests the ‘System for Rice Intensification’ technique to reduce emissions. Here, young seedlings are transplanted in a precise manner and fields are not continuously flooded during irrigation. Direct-seeded rice, where rice seeds are directly sown in fields as opposed to the traditional method of cultivation involving the transplantation of seedlings into a flooded field, is also encouraged. Drip Irrigation and the alternate wetting and drying technique — where the fields are only intermittently flooded — are also promoted.
However, they don’t address the fact that the emission reduction potentials vary widely between India’s States. Apart from large-scale demonstrations of these techniques across districts under NICRA, several country and state-level policies, like the National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture and financial incentives provided by states like Punjab and Haryana, aim to encourage the adoption of DSR and SRI. However, the reluctance to change from traditional practices, along with issues such as labour shortages, lack of technical knowledge, and changes in rainfall patterns, constrain farmers’ decisions.
Prakashan Chellattan Veettil, senior agricultural and behavioural economist at the International Rice Research Institute (IRRI), New Delhi, said a regional approach that considers the topography, soil, and irrigation conditions plus the farmers’ requirements is crucial.
Dr. Veettil is the IRRI lead in the KERA-AWD project, which is working to establish processes to cultivate low-emission rice systems in Kerala’s low-land (Thrissur kole lands) and mid-land (Palakkad Malampuzha canal) regions.
“Using a multi-pronged approach, which includes increasing soil organic carbon, understanding the hydrology and aligned irrigation systems, agricultural inputs, residue management protocols, soil microbes and farmer preferences, we are working on identifying low-emission packages, where a combination of these factors can help reduce emissions for various paddy varieties,” Dr. Veettil said.
He shared the example of lime that farmers use to neutralise the acidic soil that prevails in the State.
“We have observed that replacing a portion of the lime with [a material called] phosphogypsum has a positive impact on emissions in the subsoil surface,” he added. “We are also experimenting with different formulations and application methods for nitrogen fertilisers.”
Ashwini Chhatre, associate professor of public policy and executive director at the Bharti Institute of Public Policy, Hyderabad, said that depending on a region’s suitability for paddy, farmers should have support to opt for low-emission techniques or grow alternative local crop varieties. In his research, Dr. Chhatre found that despite facing climate-related losses and price variations, farmers still dedicate large areas of their farmlands to paddy.
“As a general strategy, there is no way forward without crop diversification,” he said. “Despite the agro-climatic diversity in the country, paddy has the largest area under cultivation at the cost of regional indigenous crops.”
Dr. Chhatre said several traditional millet varieties in Odisha have been replaced with high-yielding paddy. Reports indicate the State’s paddy fields have contributed the most to its greenhouse gas emissions.
Through initiatives like the Odisha Agriculture Drought Mitigation Programme with IRRI, and the Shree Anna Abhiyan-Odisha Millet Mission, the State government is taking steps to reduce paddy’s environmental footprint and encourage crop diversity.
“An integrated intervention for alternative grains is crucial,” Dr. Chhatre said, adding that it “must include” extension and training services, involve self-help groups, and provide procurement support. “While encouraging sustainable paddy cultivation, policies must also prevent further expansion of paddy fields to curb emissions.”
Sharmila Vaidyanathan is an independent journalist from Bengaluru.

title: Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, Galaxy Z Fold 8 and Galaxy Z Flip 8 launched
content:
Samsung on Wednesday (July 22, 2026) launched the eighth generation of foldable phones including Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, Z Fold 8 and Z Flip 8 in India. The South Korean company introduced an Ultra variant for the first time in their foldable portfolio. The launch also includes Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 and Watch 9.
These Galaxy Z8 series phones run on One UI 9 based on Android 17 out of the box. Samsung will offer 7 years of OS and 7 years of security updates to the phones.
Samsung said that Indians are using foldables for content consumption, to enhance their productivity with AI and camera.
In terms of design, Samsung has moved to flex titanium plate in the foldable phones.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra
Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra has an 8 inch QXGA+ Dynamic AMOLED 2X main display with 2504 x 2256 resolution, 422 ppi, a 120 Hz adaptive refresh rate and 3,000 nits peak brightness. It uses a 6.5 inch FHD+ Dynamic AMOLED 2X cover display with 1080 x 2520 resolution, 422 ppi and a 120 Hz adaptive refresh rate.
The book style foldable phone bears 215 grams of weight and is 4.1mm thick in unfoled and 8.9mm in folded state.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra is being protected by Corning Gorilla Glass Ceramic 3 on cover and Corning Gorilla Glass Victus 2 at the back.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra ships with a 5,000 mAh battery and supports 45W wired and 20W wireless charging. The phone is IP48 rated for water and dust protection.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra runs on Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 processor with up to 16 GB RAM and 1 TB storage.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra sports a 200 MP main camera, a 50 MP ultrawide lens and a 10 MP telephoto camera. For selfies, it has got a 10 MP lens on cover and a 10 MP camera on the main screen.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 has a 7.6 inch QXGA+ Dynamic AMOLED 2X main display with 1848 x 2448 resolution, 422 ppi, a 120 Hz adaptive refresh rate and 3,000 nits peak brightness. It uses a 5.5 inch FHD+ Dynamic AMOLED 2X cover display having 1248 x 1972 resolution, 422 ppi and a 120 Hz adaptive refresh rate.
The passport style foldable phone has 201 grams of weight. It is 4.5mm in unfolded form and 9.7mm in folded mode.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 gets Corning Gorilla Glass Ceramic 3 on the cover and Corning Gorilla Glass Victus 2 protection at the back.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 ships with a 4,800 mAh battery and supports 45W wired and 20W wireless charging. The phone is IP48 rated for water ingress.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 runs on Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 processor with up to 16 GB RAM and 1 TB storage.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 sports a 50 MP main camera and a 50 MP ultrawide lens. It uses a 10 MP lens on cover and a 10 MP camera on the main screen.
Galaxy Z Flip 8 has a 6.9 inch FHD+ Dynamic AMOLED 2X main display with 1080 x 2520 resolution, 400 ppi, a 120 Hz adaptive refresh rate and 3,000 nits peak brightness. It uses a 4.1 inch HD+ Super AMOLED cover display having 422 ppi and a 60-120 Hz adaptive refresh rate.
The Z Flip 8 claims 180 grams of weight. It measures 6.1mm in unfolded form and 13.1mm when folded.
Galaxy Z Flip 8 is being protected by Corning Gorilla Glass Victus 2 on for cover and glass fiber-reinforced polymer at the back.
Galaxy Z Flip 8 ships with a 4,300 mAh battery and supports 25W wired and 15W wireless charging. It is also IP48 rated for water protection.
Galaxy Z Flip 8 runs on Exynos 2600 processor with up to 12 GB RAM and 512 TB storage.
Galaxy Z Flip 8 sports a 50 MP main camera and a 12 MP ultrawide lens. It has a 10 MP lens for selfies.
The Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 has 1.52 inch Super AMOLED display with 5,000 nits peak brightness. Available in 47mm dial, the Watch Ultra 2 gets 2 GB RAM and 64 GB storage. The Watch Ultra 2 ships with an 800 mAh battery.
Both Watch Ultra 2 and Watch 9 use Qualcomm Snapdragon Wear Elite processor.
Galaxy Watch Ultra runs on Wear OS based on Samsung’s OS 7 and One UI 9 Watch. The Bluetooth supporting smartwatch bears IP69K rating for water resistance and is 10ATM certified.
The Galaxy Watch 9 has a 1.47 inch Super AMOLED AOD display with 3,000 nits peak brightness. It ships with a 445 mAh battery. It will be available in 40mm and 44mm dials. It also operates and run on the same OS and platform. Galaxy Watch 9 is IP68 rated and bears 5ATM certification.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra starts at ₹1,99,999 for the 12 GB/256 GB variant, ₹2,19,999 for 12 GB/512 GB and ₹2,25,999 for the 16 GB/1 TB. It will be available in Graphite, Cream, Violet Shadow and Green Shadow (online exclusive) shades.
Galaxy Z Fold 8 begins at ₹1,79,999 for the 12 GB/256 GB, ₹1,99,999 for the 12 GB/512 GB and ₹2,39,999 for 16 GB/1 TB. It will sell in Graphite, Cream, Lavender and Pistachio (online exclusive) colours.
Galaxy Z Flip 8 will be available at ₹1,24,999 for the 12 GB/256 GB and ₹1,44,999 for 12 GB/512 GB. It comes in Graphite, Cream, Pink and Mint (online exclusive) hues.
Watch Ultra 2 and Watch 9 prices will be revealed later for India market.

title: Indian boys beat Pakistan, Oman in Youth Hockey5s Asian Championship
content:
junior boys' team maintained its winning run, outplaying Pakistan and Oman in the Elite Pool of the FIH Youth Hockey5s Asian Championship in Muscat.
Against Pakistan, India prevailed 7-4, with Karan Gautam (5', 9', 19') completing a hat-trick. Rahul Yadav (3'), Prahalad Rajbhar (10'), captain Kushwaha (11') and Romit Pal (11') were the other scorers for India. Aslam Muhammad Farhan (6'), Adeel (8') and Usman Muhammad (10', 16') scored for Pakistan.
junior girls, meanwhile, were held to a 2-2 draw by China before thrashing Uzbekistan 16-0 in another Elite Pool match.
Trailing for most of the contest after Chen Ge (4') put China ahead, India fought back through Nousheen Naz (16', 17'), who scored twice to give her side the lead.
However, Jiaxin Guo (19') netted a late equaliser to earn China a share of the points.
India then rounded off the day with a dominant win over Uzbekistan, Nousheen Naz (6', 9', 15', 16') once again scoring four goals.
Sandeepa Kumari (2', 6', 20') and captain Sweety Kujur (10', 13', 20') struck hat-tricks, while Diya (3', 17') and Pushpa Manjhi (11', 13') added braces. Kiran Ekka (8') and Nilam Topno (18') also got on the scoresheet.
The tournament serves as a qualifier for the inaugural FIH Youth Hockey5s World Cup.

title: Like Tokyo Olympics, India can end five-decade World Cup medal drought: Harmanpreet
content:
Indian men's hockey captain Harmanpreet Singh believes his side is capable of ending the country's five-decade-long World Cup medal drought, saying the team heads into next month's showpiece event with the same belief and preparation that helped it end a 41-year Olympic podium wait at the Tokyo Games.
India had snapped a 41-year Olympic medal drought by winning bronze in Tokyo in 2021 before repeating the feat under Harmanpreet's captaincy at the Paris Games. The ace drag-flicker now wants the team to emulate that success at the World Cup, to be held in the Netherlands and Belgium from August 15 to 30.
"We had similar feelings as before the Tokyo Olympics. Our preparation is equally strong because our only goal is to end the 50-year World Cup podium drought," Harmanpreet said.
"Just as we won an Olympic medal after 41 years in Tokyo, we want to break the World Cup drought as well. We will go with the same mindset and the same preparation. There is no pressure; rather, it is our responsibility." India's only World Cup triumph came in Kuala Lumpur in 1975. The team had also won bronze in 1971 and silver in 1973 but has not reached the podium since.
For several members of the current squad, including those who were part of India's back-to-back Olympic bronze-medal-winning campaigns, the upcoming World Cup could be their final opportunity to make an impact on the sport's biggest stage.
"The team's performance, confidence and unity in recent months have strengthened our belief that this is a golden opportunity," said Harmanpreet, who has played 264 internationals for India.
"It is especially important for the experienced players because this could be their last World Cup, and there are expectations from them. They want to set an example through their performances and inspire the next generation." The 30-year-old, however, cautioned that success would require sustained hard work.
"The path is not easy. A lot of hard work is needed. We play with a never-say-die attitude, and that will remain our approach even in the practice sessions." Asked about India's realistic prospects, Harmanpreet said no team could be taken lightly in a tournament of such stature.
Harmanpreet said the experience would prove invaluable ahead of the World Cup.
"In the Pro League, you experiment, learn a lot and analyse opponents. We even beat teams like the Netherlands and Germany. But the World Cup is different because you don't get a second chance. Even small mistakes can prove costly," said the veteran, who also captained India at the 2023 World Cup.
flicker also expressed confidence in India's attacking unit, saying the team was no longer solely dependent on penalty corners for goals.
"In the last few matches, we have scored a lot of field goals. It doesn't matter who scores; what matters is converting opportunities. The forward line has good coordination, and I have full confidence in them." On the challenge of staying ahead as a drag-flicker in an era dominated by video analysis, Harmanpreet said innovation and attention to detail remained key.
"It is becoming more difficult because every team uses video analysis. We keep working on small aspects and study opposition penalty-corner defence closely to identify scoring opportunities." The captain also backed the youngsters set to make their World Cup debut, saying they had already gained valuable exposure against top teams through the Pro League.
"We are sharing our experience with them. As a captain, I believe in positive communication. Supporting each other and taking responsibility is crucial in major tournaments.
"The young players are not under pressure. They have already played against top teams and have been told to play their natural game while keeping the responsibilities towards the team first," he said.
rivals Pakistan in the group stage, a fixture that invariably attracts heightened attention.
"There is always hype around an India-Pakistan match, but we remind ourselves not to get carried away by emotions. Team responsibility comes first. We have done well against them in recent years and would like to continue that." The World Cup will be followed by the Asian Games in Japan, where the champions will secure direct qualification for the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics.
"I have faith in the team and our preparation. Instead of worrying about things beyond our control, we want to recover quickly after the World Cup and perform well at the Asian Games.
"We have played good hockey in Asia and want to maintain that dominance to qualify for the Olympics." Though hockey does not feature in the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow, beginning on Thursday, Harmanpreet conveyed his best wishes to the Indian contingent.
"You are representing India and obviously expectations would be high. Do your best and make the nation proud," he said.

title: Spain wins the World Cup, but did FIFA favour Argentina? | In Focus podcast
content:
Spain are world champions once again after a dominant FIFA World Cup campaign, defeating Argentina 1-0 in the final. But beyond the result, the tournament has sparked fierce debate over contrasting footballing philosophies, refereeing decisions and the narratives that shaped the competition.
Spain’s absolute domination over Argentina explained
In this episode of In Focus, we unpack Spain’s rise under Luis de la Fuente, how their possession-based game has evolved since 2010, and why they emerged as the tournament’s standout team. We also examine Argentina’s gritty run to the final, the criticism of its physical approach, and whether allegations of favourable refereeing and FIFA bias stand up to scrutiny.
Sandeep Menon, journalist and author
Edited and produced by Sharmada Venkatasubramanian

title: Possession with purpose unlocks Spain’s World Cup puzzle
content:
The oldest footballing lesson in Spain has always been deceptively simple. If you have the ball, the opponent cannot score.
For years, however, that philosophy became both its greatest gift and its greatest burden. Spain monopolised possession, accumulated passes and controlled territory, but too often it left tournaments wondering whether beautiful football alone could still win the biggest prizes. The echoes of tiki-taka lingered long after the golden generation had faded, sometimes becoming an identity that imprisoned rather than liberated.
At this World Cup, Spain did not abandon its football. It rediscovered its purpose.
Luis de la Fuente’s world champions finished the tournament with the best defence, conceding only one goal in eight matches. They dominated possession as La Roja always have, but possession was no longer the destination. It became the platform from which every other part of the game flourished. They pressed ferociously when they lost the ball, attacked vertically when spaces opened and defended with a discipline that suffocated some of the world’s finest forwards.
Against Belgium in the quarterfinals, Spain patiently waited until Mikel Merino struck the decisive late winner. Against Portugal, it accepted a tactical battle and found the goal required to make it past the Iberian neighbours. In the final, it denied Lionel Messi the fairytale ending that seemed almost scripted.
Every challenge demanded something different and Spain always had an answer.
That adaptability perhaps best explains why this team succeeded where previous Spanish sides had fallen. The Spain of Vicente del Bosque often appeared convinced that enough possession would eventually solve every problem. De la Fuente’s Spain understood that football offers many different questions.
Sometimes the answer was Lamine Yamal. Still only a teenager, Yamal entered the tournament carrying expectations that would have burdened far older footballers. His acceleration, disguised passing and fearlessness repeatedly tilted matches before opponents had organised themselves. Yet unlike so many young stars, he was never asked to carry Spain alone. Around him functioned a collective that was entirely free of hierarchy.
Rodri remained the team’s metronome, rarely hurried, forever choosing the correct pass before pressure arrived. He has now won the Player of the Tournament both in the Euro and the World Cup. Fabian Ruiz produced perhaps the finest tournament of his career, knitting midfield together while contributing goals and relentless defensive work. Nico Williams stretched defences from the opposite flank whenever he played, while Dani Olmo drifted between lines, creating overloads wherever Spain needed them. Mikel Oyarzabal provided the goals to soothe down the nerves after the initial draw with Cape Verde.
backs pushed high enough to create width without exposing the defence and goalkeeper Unai Simon was called upon surprisingly infrequently, a reflection of how effectively those in front of him protected the penalty boxes. Whenever Spain lost possession, the counter-press arrived almost instinctively, often recovering the ball before opponents could string together three passes.
Every attacker defended. Every defender initiated attacks. Possession, pressing and positional discipline became parts of the same movement rather than separate ideas.
There was also a maturity that distinguished this side from many previous champions.
Spain rarely chased matches emotionally. It never seemed rushed by the occasion, whether facing Kylian Mbappe in Dallas, Belgium in Los Angeles or Lionel Messi in the MetLife Stadium. Its football carried the serenity of a team that knew exactly who it was.
For much of the past decade, international football has been shaped by transition, athleticism and chaos. Spain reminded everyone that control still matters. But it also demonstrated that control no longer means simply completing 800 passes.
It means controlling space, tempo, emotions and ultimately the result.
In doing so, de la Fuente’s players achieved something larger than lifting the World Cup. They completed Spanish football’s evolution.

title: Neeraj Chopra, Mirabai Chanu lead India's medal hopes at CWG 2026
content:
India will begin its Commonwealth Games campaign in Glasgow on Thursday with a compact yet accomplished 125-member contingent aiming to maximise its opportunities in a drastically trimmed programme. The Games, running from July 23Aug 2, feature just 10 sports, forcing India to recalibrate expectations after a highly successful Birmingham campaign four years ago.
The Indian squad comprises 77 men and 48 women competing across eight able-bodied and five para disciplines. Athletics forms the backbone of the campaign with 32 competitors, while boxing and judo have 14 athletes each, weightlifting 12 and swimming five. Tokyo Olympic silver medallist
Mirabai Chanu will be India’s flagbearer at the opening ceremony, with Tokyo bronze medallist and former world champion boxer Lovlina Borgohain serving as baton bearer.
The overall Indian delegation comprises 190 members - 125 athletes, 51 coaches and support staff and 14 contingent officials.
Unlike Birmingham, where India fielded 210 athletes and won 61 medals, including 22 gold, Glasgow presents a very different challenge. The programme has been cut from 19 sports in 2022 to 10, with medal-rich disciplines such as badminton, wrestling, hockey, table tennis, cricket and squash omitted.
Those six sports accounted for 30 of India’s medals in Birmingham, sharply reducing the country’s traditional medal base.
down programme, India boasts several genuine gold contenders. Tokyo Olympic champion
Neeraj Chopra returns to the CWG after missing Birmingham through injury and will renew his rivalry with Pakistan’s reigning Olympic and Commonwealth champion Arshad Nadeem. The field also includes Sri Lanka’s world leader Rumesh Pathirage. Chopra, the 2018 champion, remains India’s biggest athletics hope.
Long jumper Murali Sreeshankar, a Birmingham silver medallist, and high jumper Sarvesh Kushare, who set a national record of 2.31 metres last month before finishing third at the Monaco Diamond League meeting, further strengthen India’s track and field ambitions. Other key names include Gurindervir Singh, Animesh Kujur, Parul Chaudhary, Tajinderpal Singh Toor and Gulveer Singh.
India’s weightlifting challenge will again be led by Mirabai, who is chasing a fourth CWG medal and a third successive gold. The boxing squad includes proven contenders such as Lovlina, Sakshi Chaudhary, Preeti Pawar and Jaismine Lamboria.
The contingent spans generations, with 49-year-old lawn bowler Dinesh Kumar Singh the oldest athlete and 16-year-old para cyclist Lisha Das the youngest. Competitions will be staged at Scotstoun Stadium, the Scottish Event Campus, Glasgow International Arena and Tollcross International Swimming Centre.
With Ahmedabad set to host the centenary Commonwealth Games in 2030, Glasgow offers India not only a shot at medals but also a valuable learning opportunity before it welcomes the sporting community home.
Shooting (63 gold medals won by India tilll date), table tennis (10 gold), wrestling (100+ overall medals, including 6 gold in 2022 Birmingham Games), field hockey (3 silver for men while women won gold in 2002, silver in 2006 and bronze in 2022), squash (women’s doubles gold in Glasgow 2014), women’s cricket (silver in 2022).
Why are these sport excluded?
Glasgow stepped in after the Australian state of Victoria withdrew citing escalating costs. Organisers trimmed the Games to 10 sports across four venues within an eight-mile radius to keep expenses under control. Victoria’s projected budget had ballooned to around 14,500 cr. Glasgow will operate on a leaner £150 million (around 1,750 cr) budget, with £100 million funded by the Commonwealth Sport.

title: 'I'm sorry': Why Argentina's assistant coach apologised after FIFA World Cup final
content:
Argentina assistant coach Roberto Ayala has apologised for his altercation with Spain midfielder Dani Olmo after the FIFA World Cup 2026 final, admitting that his actions were unacceptable. The ugly scenes unfolded after Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 in extra time on Sunday to lift the World Cup title.
match brawl.
Chaos after the final whistle
Tensions boiled over immediately after the final whistle as players and staff from both teams became involved in heated confrontations.
Spain captain Rodri was seen arguing with Argentina defender Nicolas Otamendi. Leandro Paredes was shown a red card after punching Gavi, who had run onto the pitch to celebrate Spain's victory.
Ayala also became involved in the incident.
Television footage showed the former Argentina defender approaching Dani Olmo before making contact with the Spain midfielder. Olmo chose not to retaliate.
Ayala apologises for his actions
Speaking after the incident, Ayala admitted he had made a mistake and said he regretted losing control.
"Obviously I'm sorry. Because of my position, I can't allow a feeling, or what I might receive from the other party, to change my mood and my actions.
I'm sorry, but for me, these are things that stay there and that's it," Ayala said in an interview with Valencia Capital Radio.
"For me, things need to be put behind us and left there. It was more of a push than anything else, it wasn't a punch like they're saying, and that's it. It was a reaction to something he said, but that's it. If I see him, I'll obviously apologise to him in person," he said.
Ayala said emotions got the better of him during the chaotic scenes and accepted full responsibility for his behaviour.
"It's a shame, and you have to accept the facts and the things you've done on the field. When the game ends, what I see is a fight in the middle of the pitch, and we rush to go after our players, and it ends up like that because that's not who we are," he said.
He added that his original intention was to stop the confrontation rather than become part of it.
"I take responsibility for what I did. My intention was to go and separate them, that was my intention, but sometimes things happen and emotions run high, but that's no excuse. And given my position, my behaviour has to be different, regardless of what I receive," he said.
match brawl.
The governing body's disciplinary committee has appointed a disciplinary and ethics prosecutor to investigate the incidents involving players and coaching staff.

title: Kuldeep Yadav lands county deal after no-show in India vs England ODIs
content:
India spinner Kuldeep Yadav has signed with Yorkshire as an overseas player for the remainder of the English domestic season. The left-arm wrist-spinner will play five One-Day Cup matches before returning in September for three County Championship games.
His stint begins with Friday's 50-over clash against Glamorgan.
In the club's statement, Kuldeep expressed his delight at joining one of England's most historic county clubs, saying, "I’m really pleased to be joining Yorkshire and grateful for this opportunity. It will be a real privilege to represent a club with Yorkshire’s proud history."
He also said he is looking forward to testing himself in English conditions again.
“I’ve always enjoyed the challenge of playing in English conditions, and after speaking with the management, I became really excited to join up with the team and I’m looking forward to making a positive contribution during my time at Headingley," he added.
The move comes shortly after India's ODI series in England, where Kuldeep did not feature in any of the three matches despite being part of the squad.
Yorkshire believe Kuldeep's arrival will strengthen their bowling attack, especially with spin expected to play a bigger role during the closing stages of the season.
General manager of cricket Gavin Hamilton said, "Kuldeep is an accomplished, international-quality player who will add incredible strength to our lineup for these blocks of fixtures."
He added, "Given the dry summer we’ve experienced, we expect spin to play an important role during the closing stages of the season. Kuldeep gives us another dimension with his left-arm wrist spin and brings a wealth of experience at the highest level. We’re delighted to welcome him to Yorkshire."
Kuldeep joins a growing list of Indian cricketers featuring in county cricket this season, including Yuzvendra Chahal, Jaydev Unadkat, Manav Suthar, R. Sai Kishore and Ashutosh Sharma.

title: Trump wants Infantino as next UN secretary-general: Report
content:
general of the United Nations, according to a report by the New York Post.
The report said Trump was impressed by Infantino's role in organising the
FIFA World Cup and believes the Swiss official has the ability to unite people across the world.
Trump backs Infantino for top UN role
According to the report, Trump believes Infantino would be a strong candidate to replace current UN secretary-general António Guterres, whose term ends in December.
"He is respected by everyone around the world, and recognizes he has a special ability to bring people together."
However, it is still unclear whether Infantino is interested in the position or whether Trump has discussed the proposal with him.
general, a candidate must first receive the backing of the 15-member UN Security Council, where the five permanent members have veto power, before being approved by the UN General Assembly.
Trump and Infantino grew closer during the World Cup
The report said Infantino and Trump developed a close relationship while working together during this year's FIFA World Cup.
Infantino reportedly made special efforts to include Trump during the tournament and had earlier presented him with FIFA's inaugural Peace Prize in December.
World Cup final drew boos for Trump and Infantino
Trump and Infantino were also in the spotlight during the FIFA World Cup final between Spain and Argentina.
Trump arrived at the stadium aboard Marine One and watched the match from a VIP suite alongside Infantino. According to The Guardian, he received scattered boos when he appeared on the stadium's giant screens before kick-off.
The reaction became louder after the match when Trump and Infantino walked onto the field for the trophy presentation following Spain's 1-0 victory.
The atmosphere turned more awkward when Trump remained on the podium after handing over the trophy, appearing to join Spain's celebrations before eventually leaving the stage.
Trump's close relationship with Infantino also came under scrutiny earlier in the tournament after USA forward Folarin Balogun's one-match suspension for a red card was lifted ahead of the Round of 16 clash against Belgium.
